preamble: The phonetic transformation from the voiced palatal approximant /j/ to the voiceless glottal fricative /h/ is a documented sound change, primarily occurring through a process known as debuccalization. This involves the weakening of an oral consonant, where its place of articulation shifts to the glottis, often accompanied by a loss of voicing and a change in manner from approximant to fricative.
phoneticEffects: Debuccalization, lenition, loss of oral gestural targets, shift in place of articulation from palatal to glottal, change in manner from approximant to fricative, devoicing

languageExamples.note: In the standard variety of Yoruba, the glide /j/ undergoes debuccalization to the glottal fricative /h/ before nasalized homorganic vowels, often in intervocalic contexts.
In Proto-Greek, initial *j shifted to /h/ or was replaced by /z/ (possibly [dz]).
The loss of the /j/ to /h/ phonemic contrast is a primary phonological change defining Proto-Cholan.

examples.note: The /j/ sound in borrowings like 'Yovsep' (from Hebrew Yosef) became /h/ in Armenian.

sources: The Un-mouthing of Sounds - Danny L. Bate (December 08 2020).
Debuccalization and supplementary gestures - Jeremy O'Brien (November 20 2010).
THE EVOLUTION OF ANCIENT MAYA EXCHANGE SYSTEMS: AN ETYMOLOGICAL STUDY OF ECONOMIC VOCABULARY IN THE MAYAN LANGUAGE FAMILY - Cambridge University Press (August 07 2014).
PHENOMENON: OVERGENERALISED OR CONFUSED? THE NIGERIAN YORUBA-ENGLISH SPEAKERS AS A CASE STUDY - SciSpace.
